text: Precise control over the excited-state dynamics of first-row transition metal complexes is a long-standing challenge in inorganic photochemistry. While $d^6$ complexes based on precious metals like Ru(II), Os(II), and Ir(III) are cornerstones of modern photocatalysis and solar energy conversion, their earth-abundant 3$d^6$ counterparts typically suffer from ultrafast non-radiative deactivation via low-lying metal-centered states. Here, we report the development of Cr(0) tris(diisocyanide) complexes that overcome these limitations through targeted molecular design. By employing chelate ligands that provide strong ligand fields and structural rigidity, we achieved metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) excited-state lifetimes of up to 47 ns and photoluminescence quantum yields exceeding 1% in solution at room temperature. These photophysical properties are directly competitive with benchmark Os(II) complexes. Leveraging their exceptionally high reducing power in the excited state ($E_{ox}^* \approx -2.7$ V vs. $\text{Fc}^{+/0}$), we demonstrate that these Cr(0) luminophores can drive demanding photoredox transformations, including the reductive dehalogenation of aryl halides and overall redox-neutral C–C coupling reactions under red-light irradiation. This work establishes a viable strategy for replacing noble metals with abundant first-row transition metals in MLCT-based photochemical applications.
